course_info.title: British Literature I
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Old English Literature: Beowulf and the Heroic Tradition
learning_objectives: Understand the oral-formulaic composition of Old English poetry
Identify the heroic values expressed in Beowulf: loyalty, courage, fate, and fame
Analyze the tension between pagan and Christian elements in the poem
readings: Norton Anthology of English Literature, Old English period introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: British literature begins before Britain as we know it existed. The Old English period produced a body of poetry rooted in oral tradition, heroic values, and a world view shaped by both Germanic paganism and emerging Christianity.
key_concepts: Old English as a distinct language requiring translation for modern readers
Oral-formulaic composition: kennings, alliteration, and the half-line structure
The comitatus bond: the warrior's loyalty to his lord as the foundation of heroic society
Wyrd (fate) and the elegiac tone of Old English poetry
The Beowulf manuscript: a single surviving copy and its implications for literary history
lecture_notes.demonstration: Listening to a recording of lines 1-25 in Old English followed by Heaney's translation, then analyzing how the original alliterative structure works
setup_requirements: Norton Anthology of English Literature, Vol. A
Heaney translation of Beowulf (or anthology version)

review.value: A compressed metaphorical compound used in Old English poetry: 'whale-road' for the sea, 'bone-house' for the body, 'ring-giver' for the king
The bond of loyalty between a lord and his warriors, the central social and moral relationship in heroic poetry, the violation of which constitutes the gravest dishonor

class_schedule.topic: Medieval Romance: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
learning_objectives: Identify the conventions of medieval romance: quest, trial, courtly love, marvel
Analyze the poem's exploration of honor, temptation, and human imperfection
Understand the relationship between romance conventions and the poem's moral argument
readings: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, Simon Armitage translation (complete)

lecture_notes.introduction: Medieval romance is not romance in the modern sense. It is a genre of adventure, marvel, and moral testing. Sir Gawain and the Green Knight uses the conventions of romance to explore questions about honor, honesty, and the limits of human virtue.
key_concepts: Romance conventions: the quest, the marvel, the test, the return
The pentangle: five sets of five virtues symbolizing Gawain's supposed perfection
The exchange of winnings: a game within the game that becomes the real test
The Green Knight as both challenger and judge, nature and supernature
The poem's ambiguous ending: Gawain's shame vs. the court's laughter
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of the temptation scenes in Fitt III, analyzing how the lady's seduction attempts mirror the hunting scenes and what the parallel structure reveals about the poem's moral framework
setup_requirements: Complete text of Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
Map of the poem's structure (four fitts)

review.value: A five-pointed star symbolizing Gawain's fivefold perfection: five senses, five fingers, five wounds of Christ, five joys of Mary, and five knightly virtues

The game between Gawain and Bertilak in which they agree to exchange whatever they gain each day — the real moral test of the poem, where Gawain's failure to surrender the green girdle reveals his imperfection

class_schedule.topic: Chaucer and the Canterbury Tales: Voice, Satire, and Social Critique
learning_objectives: Identify Chaucer's use of the frame narrative and the relationship between teller and tale
Analyze Chaucer's satirical portraits of medieval social types in the General Prologue
Understand Middle English as a transitional language and Chaucer's role in establishing English as a literary language
readings: Chaucer, The Canterbury Tales: General Prologue and The Wife of Bath's Prologue and Tale

lecture_notes.introduction: Chaucer is the first great English poet to write in English rather than French or Latin. The Canterbury Tales is a masterpiece of voice, irony, and social observation — a portrait of an entire society told through the stories its members tell about themselves.
key_concepts: The frame narrative: a pilgrimage that justifies gathering all social classes together
The relationship between teller and tale: how each pilgrim's story reflects their character and preoccupations
Chaucer's ironic narrator: appearing naive while delivering devastating social critique
The Wife of Bath: proto-feminist or misogynist caricature — the critical debate
Estates satire: literary tradition of critiquing the three orders of medieval society
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of three General Prologue portraits — the Knight, the Prioress, and the Pardoner — analyzing how Chaucer uses ironic detail to praise, undercut, or condemn each figure
setup_requirements: Canterbury Tales text with modern English facing-page translation
Social context handout on medieval estates and pilgrimage

review.value: A medieval literary tradition that critiques society by examining representatives of the three estates: those who fight (nobility), those who pray (clergy), and those who work (commoners)
Chaucer's narrator appears to admire everyone he describes, but the specific details he chooses reveal the gap between appearance and reality, self-image and truth

class_schedule.topic: The English Renaissance: Spenser and the Sonnet Tradition
learning_objectives: Trace the development of the English sonnet from Petrarch through Spenser and Shakespeare
Analyze how sonnet conventions both enable and constrain expression of desire and devotion
Understand the Renaissance concept of imitation as creative transformation, not copying
readings: Norton Anthology, Early Modern period introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: The sonnet reached England from Italy via Wyatt and Surrey, and in the hands of Spenser and Shakespeare it became the dominant lyric form of the English Renaissance. Understanding its conventions — and how poets manipulate them — is essential to reading Renaissance poetry.
key_concepts: The Petrarchan tradition: idealized beloved, suffering lover, blazon, conceit
Spenserian sonnet form: ABAB BCBC CDCD EE — interlocking rhyme creating continuity
Shakespearean sonnet form: ABAB CDCD EFEF GG — three quatrains building to a couplet turn
Anti-Petrarchan subversion: Shakespeare's Sonnet 130 as critique of conventional praise
Renaissance imitation: transforming a model rather than merely copying it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side comparison of Spenser's Sonnet 75 and Shakespeare's Sonnet 18, both addressing the power of poetry to immortalize the beloved, analyzing how different sonnet structures create different arguments
setup_requirements: Printed copies of all assigned sonnets

review.value: A poetic catalogue praising the beloved's body part by part, conventionally comparing each to precious objects: eyes like stars, lips like coral, skin like snow
The 'turn' in a sonnet — the shift in argument, tone, or perspective, typically occurring at line 9 in the Petrarchan form or at the final couplet in the Shakespearean form

class_schedule.topic: Milton and Paradise Lost: Epic Ambition and Theological Argument
learning_objectives: Identify the conventions of classical epic that Milton adapts for Christian purposes
Analyze Milton's characterization of Satan as simultaneously compelling and condemned
Understand the poem's central argument: 'to justify the ways of God to men'
readings: Milton, Paradise Lost, Books I and IX
Norton Anthology, Milton introduction and headnotes

lecture_notes.introduction: Paradise Lost is the great English epic — an attempt to rival Homer and Virgil while retelling the Christian story of the Fall. Its most enduring interpretive problem is Satan: why does Milton make the devil so magnificent?
key_concepts: Epic conventions adapted: invocation, in medias res, catalogue, epic simile
Blank verse: Milton's choice of unrhymed iambic pentameter for epic dignity
Satan's rhetoric: persuasive, self-dramatizing, and ultimately self-deceiving
The Romantic reading: Blake's claim that Milton was 'of the Devil's party without knowing it'
Free will and the fortunate fall: the poem's theological argument
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of Satan's opening speech in Book I ('Is this the region, this the soil, the clime...'), analyzing how Milton constructs a speaker who is both heroic and deluded
setup_requirements: Paradise Lost, Books I and IX (annotated edition recommended)
Timeline of Milton's life and political context

review.value: An extended comparison that develops over several lines and often introduces material that complicates or enriches the primary narrative — Milton's similes frequently undercut Satan's grandeur
The theological argument that Adam's sin, though evil, ultimately led to a greater good — the redemption of humanity through Christ — making the Fall 'fortunate' in retrospect

class_schedule.topic: The Restoration and Eighteenth Century: Satire and Reason
learning_objectives: Analyze the satirical methods of Pope and Swift
Understand the Augustan literary values: wit, decorum, balance, and classical imitation
Identify the social and political targets of Restoration and eighteenth-century satire
readings: Pope, The Rape of the Lock, Cantos I-III

lecture_notes.introduction: The Restoration and eighteenth century prized reason, wit, and social order. Its greatest literary achievement is satire — using irony, parody, and mock-heroic form to expose human folly, vanity, and corruption.
key_concepts: Mock-heroic: applying epic conventions to trivial subjects to expose their absurdity
Horatian vs. Juvenalian satire: gentle correction vs. savage indignation
The heroic couplet: Pope's mastery of the balanced, antithetical rhyming pair
Swift's ironic persona: the apparently reasonable voice proposing monstrous solutions
The satirist's paradox: attacking society while depending on society's values to make the attack legible
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing Pope's description of Belinda's dressing table as an altar in The Rape of the Lock, showing how mock-heroic elevation simultaneously celebrates and mocks vanity
setup_requirements: Historical context handout on Augustan literary culture

review.value: A literary technique that applies the elevated style, conventions, and machinery of epic poetry to trivial subjects, creating comedy through the disjunction between form and content

In 'A Modest Proposal,' the speaker is not Swift but a reasonable-sounding projector whose calm economic logic about eating babies exposes the real dehumanization of the Irish by English colonial policy

questions.question: What is a kenning? Provide two examples from Beowulf and explain what each one reveals about the Anglo-Saxon world view.
questions.answer: A kenning is a compressed metaphorical compound used in Old English poetry. 'Whale-road' for the sea reveals a world view in which the ocean is understood as a path for travel and adventure. 'Ring-giver' for king reveals a social system in which a lord's primary obligation is generosity to his warriors — the distribution of treasure that binds the comitatus together.

rubric.key: Explains what each reveals about Anglo-Saxon culture

questions.question: Explain the moral significance of the 'exchange of winnings' game in Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. Why is Gawain's failure in this game more important than his confrontation with the Green Knight?
questions.answer: The exchange of winnings game tests Gawain's honesty and integrity in everyday social situations, not his physical courage in a heroic confrontation. Gawain's failure to surrender the green girdle reveals that even the most virtuous knight is imperfect when self-preservation conflicts with honor. The poem suggests that moral perfection in small, private moments is harder than heroic bravery in public ones.

rubric.key: Identifies the game as a test of honesty over courage
Explains the specific failure with the green girdle
Connects to the poem's larger argument about human imperfection

questions.question: Compare the structure of the Spenserian sonnet with the Shakespearean sonnet. How does each structure shape the development of the poem's argument?
questions.answer: The Spenserian sonnet (ABAB BCBC CDCD EE) uses interlocking rhymes that create continuity between quatrains, producing a flowing, cumulative argument. The Shakespearean sonnet (ABAB CDCD EFEF GG) uses distinct quatrains that each develop a separate facet of the argument, building to a concluding couplet that delivers a summary, reversal, or epigrammatic turn.

rubric.key: Correct rhyme schemes for both forms
Explains how Spenserian interlocking creates continuity
Explains how Shakespearean structure builds to a couplet turn

questions.question: William Blake wrote that Milton was 'of the Devil's party without knowing it.' What is the interpretive problem Blake is identifying, and how might a defender of Milton respond?
questions.answer: Blake is noting that Satan is the most compelling character in Paradise Lost — eloquent, passionate, and psychologically complex — while God and the angels are comparatively flat. This makes Satan seem like the poem's true hero. A defender of Milton might argue that Satan's rhetorical power is precisely the point: Milton demonstrates how evil seduces through beautiful language, and the careful reader recognizes that Satan's speeches, while magnificent, are consistently self-serving and self-deceiving.

rubric.key: Identifies the problem: Satan is more compelling than God
Provides a coherent counter-argument defending Milton's intention

major_assignments.title: Period Analysis Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,000-2,500 word essay analyzing how a single literary work from the period covered (Old English through the eighteenth century) reflects or challenges the values of its historical moment. The essay must integrate at least two secondary scholarly sources.
objectives: Demonstrate the ability to read a literary text within its historical and cultural context
Construct an argument about the relationship between a text and its period
Integrate primary textual evidence with secondary scholarly sources
Write clear, thesis-driven academic prose in MLA format
deliverables: Works Cited page with primary text and at least 2 secondary scholarly sources
Annotated bibliography submitted as a separate document

criteria: Thesis makes a specific, arguable claim about text and historical context
Argument develops logically and avoids anachronistic reading
Historical context illuminates the text rather than replacing close reading
Conclusion extends the argument beyond summary
Close reading of primary text is detailed and accurate
Historical context is specific and well-sourced, not vague generalization
Relationship between text and context is argued, not assumed
Secondary sources are integrated to support the argument, not merely cited
Prose is clear, precise, and free of errors
MLA format is followed correctly throughout
Quotations are integrated smoothly with proper citation
Essay meets length requirements without padding
Strong thesis connecting literary text to its historical moment
Detailed close reading integrated with specific historical evidence
Effective use of secondary scholarly sources
Clear, well-organized academic prose in correct MLA format

period_analysis_essay.description: Essay analyzing a text within its historical and cultural context

close_reading_responses.description: Weekly close reading responses demonstrating engagement with assigned texts

quizzes.description: Quizzes assessing knowledge of literary history, terminology, and assigned readings

midterm_examination.description: Identification, passage analysis, and short essay covering medieval through Renaissance

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all periods studied with passage analysis and comparative essay

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. This is a lecture-discussion course; more than 3 unexcused absences will result in a full letter grade deduction. Active participation in discussion is expected and assessed.
course_policies.late_submissions: Essays are penalized 5% per day late. Reading responses are not accepted late. Extensions require advance arrangement at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work must be original individual work. Discussion of texts with classmates is encouraged, but written submissions must be independently produced. Cite all sources.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, including uncited paraphrase and AI-generated text submitted as your own, will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Norton Anthology of English Literature (Volume A recommended), word processing software, university library database access, and LMS access.
